# Part 3 — Mapping Buttons to Directions {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #00C9A7)"}

## The Logic: `switch` Statements

Instead of using many `if` statements, we use a `switch` to handle many buttons easily.

```cpp
void handleCommand(unsigned long cmd) {
  switch (cmd) {
    case 0xFF18E7: // Replace with YOUR 'UP' code
      forward();
      break;
    case 0xFF4AB5: // Replace with YOUR 'DOWN' code
      backward();
      break;
    case 0xFF38C7: // Replace with YOUR 'OK' code
      stopCar();
      break;
  }
}
```

## Setup & Loop

We need to tell the robot to "listen" at the start and "resume" after every signal.

```cpp
#include <IRremote.hpp>
#define IR_RECEIVE_PIN 2

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  IrReceiver.begin(IR_RECEIVE_PIN, ENABLE_LED_FEEDBACK);
  // ... usual motor setup goes here ...
}

void loop() {
  if (IrReceiver.decode()) {
    unsigned long cmd = IrReceiver.decodedIRData.decodedRawData;
    Serial.println(cmd, HEX); 
    
    handleCommand(cmd); // Run our logic
    
    IrReceiver.resume(); // Ready for next signal
  }
}
```

## Brainstorming: The "Stickiness" Problem

::: {.callout-warning}
## Problem
If you press "Forward" once, the robot starts moving. But if you let go, it **keeps moving**! Why?
:::

- The IR remote only sends a signal when it's pressed.
- The Robot doesn't know you let go unless you tell it.
- **Solution Ideas:**
  1. Add a dedicated "Stop" button.
  2. Use a timer (if no signal for 0.5s, stop).
  3. (Advanced) Check for the "Repeat" code (`0xFFFFFFFF`).
